title: Finding Key Details
introBody: Every story has important clues called key details. They tell us who is in the story, what happens, and where it takes place. Today we will learn how to find them!
introImage: A happy cartoon child sitting cross-legged on a rainbow rug, holding a big colorful storybook with sparkly stars floating out of the pages.
teachBody: Key details are the important parts of a story. They answer questions like: Who is the story about? What happened? Where did it happen? Finding key details helps us understand the story better!
teachImage: A friendly cartoon magnifying glass character with big eyes examining a giant open book, with glowing golden words highlighted on the pages.
exampleBody: Here is a story: Max the dog ran to the park. He played with a red ball. The key details are: Who? Max the dog. Where? The park. What did he do? He played with a red ball!
exampleImage: A joyful cartoon dog running through a sunny park chasing a bouncing red ball, with green grass and a bright blue sky.
tipBody: After you read, ask yourself three questions: Who is in the story? What happened? Where did it happen? These questions help you find the key details every time!
tipImage: Three large friendly cartoon question marks in red, blue, and green, each wearing a detective hat, floating above an open book.
practiceBody: Great job learning about key details! Now let's practice finding key details in some short stories. You can do this!
practiceImage: An excited cartoon child jumping with arms raised in celebration, surrounded by gold stars and colorful confetti, with a big thumbs-up sign nearby.

title: Retelling Stories
introBody: When we retell a story, we say what happened in our own words. We tell the beginning, the middle, and the end. It is like telling a friend about a great story you just read!
introImage: Two cartoon children sitting together on a bench, one animatedly telling a story with hand gestures while the other listens with wide excited eyes.
teachHeading: How Do We Retell a Story?
teachBody: To retell a story, think about what happened first, next, and last. Use your own words to share the most important parts. Remember to include the characters and what they did!
teachImage: Three colorful stepping stones labeled "First," "Next," and "Last" forming a path through a garden, with a small cartoon rabbit hopping along them.
exampleBody: A story says: A bird built a nest. Then she laid three eggs. At last, the baby birds hatched! We can retell it: First, a bird built a nest. Next, she laid eggs. Last, the babies hatched!
exampleImage: A smiling cartoon mother bird sitting on a nest in a tree with three baby birds poking their heads out of cracked eggs.
tipBody: Use the words "first," "next," and "last" when you retell a story. These words help you put everything in the right order so your retelling makes sense!
tipImage: A cheerful cartoon hand holding up three fingers, each finger wearing a tiny hat labeled "First," "Next," and "Last" in bright colors.
practiceBody: Awesome work! Now let's practice retelling some short stories. Remember to tell what happened in order. You are going to do great!
practiceImage: A confident cartoon child standing at a small podium with a microphone, retelling a story to a group of smiling stuffed animals sitting in tiny chairs.

title: Characters, Settings, and Events
introBody: Every story has characters, a setting, and events. Characters are who the story is about. The setting is where it happens. Events are what happens. Let's learn to spot them all!
introImage: A cartoon castle on a hill with a princess, a dragon, and a knight standing in front of it under a sunny sky with fluffy clouds.
teachBody: Characters can be people or animals in a story. The setting is the place and time, like a school or a beach. Events are the things that happen, like a race or a party!
teachImage: Three large colorful puzzle pieces fitting together, one showing a cartoon cat, one showing a cozy house, and one showing a birthday cake with candles.
exampleBody: Here is a story: Lily played at the beach. She built a sandcastle. The character is Lily. The setting is the beach. The event is that she built a sandcastle!
exampleImage: A smiling cartoon girl with a sun hat kneeling on a sandy beach, building a tall sandcastle with a little flag on top, waves in the background.
tipBody: When you read a story, ask: Who is in it? That is the character. Where are they? That is the setting. What do they do? That is the event!
tipImage: Three friendly cartoon owls sitting on a branch, each holding a sign that reads "Who?," "Where?," and "What happened?" in colorful letters.
practiceBody: You are doing so well! Now let's practice finding characters, settings, and events in some stories. I know you can do it!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a safari hat and holding binoculars, searching through a giant storybook with characters and settings popping out of the pages.

title: Discovering New Words
introBody: Sometimes when we read, we find words we do not know yet. That is okay! We can use clues in the story to figure out what new words mean. It is like being a word detective!
introImage: A cartoon child wearing an oversized detective hat and holding a magnifying glass over a big sparkly word floating in the air.
teachHeading: How Do We Figure Out New Words?
teachBody: When you see a word you do not know, look at the words around it for clues. You can also look at the pictures. The story can help you guess what the new word means!
teachImage: A giant open book with one glowing word in the center, and colorful arrows pointing to it from the surrounding words and a picture on the opposite page.
exampleBody: A story says: The bunny was famished, so he ate five carrots. What does "famished" mean? The bunny ate a lot of carrots, so "famished" must mean very hungry!
exampleImage: A very hungry cartoon bunny sitting at a table piled high with orange carrots, eagerly munching with crumbs around its mouth.
tipBody: If you find a tricky word, read the sentence again. Look at what is happening in the story. The clues around the word will help you understand it!
tipImage: A cartoon lightbulb character with a big smile turning on above an open book, with tiny word clues floating around it like fireflies.
practiceBody: Wonderful! Now let's practice figuring out new words using clues from stories. You are becoming a great word detective!
practiceImage: A proud cartoon child wearing a detective badge, standing next to a board with new vocabulary words connected by colorful string like a detective board.

title: Types of Stories
introHeading: Let's Learn About Types of Books!
introBody: Did you know there are different kinds of books? Some books tell stories and some have poems. Learning to tell them apart makes reading even more fun!
introImage: A colorful cartoon bookshelf filled with different types of books, including a storybook with a dragon on the cover and a poetry book with a heart on the cover.
teachBody: A storybook tells a story with characters and events from beginning to end. A poem uses special words that sometimes rhyme and can be short and fun to read aloud!
teachImage: A cartoon storybook standing upright on the left with characters walking out of it, and a scroll of poetry on the right with musical notes floating above it.
exampleBody: "Once upon a time, a frog found a crown" is from a storybook. "Roses are red, violets are blue" is a poem because it rhymes! Can you tell the difference?
exampleImage: A cartoon frog wearing a crown on one side and a bouquet of red roses and blue violets on the other side, separated by a decorative line.
tipBody: Storybooks usually start with words like "Once upon a time." Poems are often shorter and have words that sound alike at the end of each line!
tipImage: A cartoon signpost at a fork in a road, one arrow pointing to "Storybooks" with a castle icon and the other pointing to "Poems" with a musical note icon.
practiceBody: Great job! Now let's practice telling storybooks and poems apart. Pay attention to how the words look and sound. You have got this!
practiceImage: A cartoon child happily sorting books into two colorful bins, one labeled "Stories" and the other labeled "Poems," with books flying into the correct bins.

title: Authors and Illustrators
introHeading: Let's Learn About Authors and Illustrators!
introBody: Every book is made by special people. The author writes the words and the illustrator draws the pictures. Together they make the books we love to read!
introImage: A cartoon author sitting at a desk writing with a feather pen on one side, and a cartoon illustrator painting on a canvas on the other side, with a book between them.
teachBody: The author is the person who writes the story. The illustrator is the person who draws the pictures. Sometimes one person does both jobs!
teachImage: A friendly cartoon book character pointing to its front cover, with a label showing "Written by" with a pencil icon and "Illustrated by" with a paintbrush icon.
exampleBody: Look at a book cover. It says "Written by Sam Lee" and "Illustrated by Mia Chen." Sam Lee is the author who wrote the words. Mia Chen is the illustrator who drew the pictures!
exampleImage: A large cartoon book cover showing a friendly bear, with "Written by Sam Lee" in blue text and "Illustrated by Mia Chen" in green text clearly visible.
tipBody: Look at the cover of a book. The author's name is usually near the title. Remember: author equals words, illustrator equals pictures!
tipImage: A cartoon pencil character labeled "Author" shaking hands with a cartoon paintbrush character labeled "Illustrator" in front of a completed storybook.
practiceBody: Nice work! Now let's practice finding authors and illustrators. Remember who writes the words and who draws the pictures!
practiceImage: A cartoon child holding up a book and pointing to the author and illustrator names on the cover with a proud smile, with gold stars around them.

title: Pictures Tell the Story
introBody: The pictures in a book are called illustrations. They help tell the story along with the words. Pictures can show us things the words do not say!
introImage: A giant open storybook with vivid illustrations of a forest scene popping out in 3D, with a cartoon child peering into the pictures with amazement.
teachBody: Illustrations show us what characters look like, how they feel, and where they are. When you look at pictures and read the words together, you understand the story even better!
teachImage: A split scene showing a line of text saying "The cat was scared" on top, and below it an illustration of a wide-eyed cartoon cat with fur standing up.
exampleBody: The words say: "Ben went outside." The picture shows Ben wearing a raincoat and holding an umbrella. The picture tells us it is raining, even though the words do not say that!
exampleImage: A cartoon boy in a bright yellow raincoat holding a red umbrella, standing in puddles with raindrops falling around him and a gray cloudy sky above.
tipBody: Always look at the pictures when you read! They give you extra clues about the story. Ask yourself: What does the picture show me that the words did not say?
tipImage: A cartoon pair of big friendly eyes with a sparkle, looking closely at an open picture book, with little clue bubbles floating up from the illustration.
practiceBody: Fantastic! Now let's practice using pictures to understand stories better. Look carefully at every picture for extra clues!
practiceImage: A cartoon child with a big smile holding a picture book open wide, with colorful illustrations glowing and little detail arrows pointing to parts of the picture.

title: Comparing Characters
introBody: Sometimes we read two stories and notice the characters do similar or different things. Comparing characters means finding what is the same and what is different about them!
introImage: Two cartoon animals standing side by side, a brave lion and a shy turtle, with a big "VS" in sparkly letters between them.
teachBody: When we compare characters, we look at what they both do and what is different. One character might be brave and another might be shy. Both might love their friends!
teachImage: A large Venn diagram made of two overlapping colorful circles, with cartoon character faces in each section and a smiley face in the overlapping middle.
exampleBody: In one story, a cat climbs a tree to save a bird. In another story, a dog digs a hole to find a bone. Both animals are on adventures, but they do different things!
exampleImage: A cartoon cat up in a tree reaching for a little bird on one side, and a cartoon dog happily digging a hole on the other side, with a dotted line between them.
tipBody: Ask yourself: What did both characters do? What was different? Thinking about same and different helps you compare characters like a pro!
tipImage: Two cartoon thinking bubbles side by side, one with a checkmark labeled "Same" and one with an X labeled "Different," floating above a smiling child.
practiceBody: You are a star! Now let's practice comparing characters from different stories. Look for what is the same and what is different!
practiceImage: Two cartoon storybook characters reaching out of their own books to shake hands across the gap, with sparkles where their hands meet.

title: Key Details in Facts
introHeading: Let's Find Key Details in Facts!
introBody: Some books teach us real facts about the world. These books have key details too! Key details in fact books tell us important information about a topic.
introImage: A cartoon child wearing big round glasses, sitting at a desk with a book about animals open in front of them, with real animal facts floating up from the pages.
teachBody: When we read a fact book, key details are the most important pieces of information. They answer questions like: What is it? What does it do? Where does it live?
teachImage: A cartoon clipboard with a checklist of key detail questions, each with a bright checkmark, sitting next to an open book about the ocean with fish illustrations.
exampleBody: A book says: Penguins live in cold places. They eat fish. They cannot fly. The key details are: Where do they live? Cold places. What do they eat? Fish. Can they fly? No!
exampleImage: A group of adorable cartoon penguins standing on an icy shore, one holding a fish, with snowflakes gently falling and a blue ocean behind them.
tipBody: When you read a fact book, ask: What did I learn? The answers are the key details. Try to remember the most important facts!
tipImage: A cartoon brain character flexing its muscles and smiling, surrounded by floating fact bubbles with icons of animals, plants, and the sun.
practiceBody: Super work! Now let's practice finding key details in fact passages. Read carefully and look for the important information!
practiceImage: A cartoon child giving a big thumbs up next to a pile of colorful fact books about animals, space, and nature, with stars twinkling around them.

title: Finding the Main Topic
introBody: Every fact book is mostly about one big thing. That big thing is called the main topic. Learning to find it helps us understand what the whole book is about!
introImage: A large cartoon magnifying glass hovering over an open book, with the main topic glowing brightly in the center surrounded by smaller detail words.
teachBody: The main topic is what the whole book or passage is mostly about. It is the big idea. All the key details connect back to the main topic!
teachImage: A cartoon sun in the center with rays going out to smaller icons of related details, like a sun as the main topic with planets as the details orbiting around it.
exampleBody: A book talks about apples being red or green, growing on trees, and tasting sweet. What is the main topic? Apples! All the details are about apples.
exampleImage: A big, shiny cartoon apple tree loaded with red and green apples, with a basket of apples at the base and a small label that says "Apples" in cheerful letters.
tipBody: Ask yourself: What is this book mostly about? If most of the sentences are about the same thing, that thing is the main topic!
tipImage: A cartoon child with a thought bubble above their head containing a big glowing star labeled "Main Topic" with smaller stars around it labeled "Details."
practiceBody: Great learning! Now let's practice finding the main topic in some passages. Look for the big idea that everything connects to!
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing on top of a large open book, holding a flag that says "Main Topic" and planting it proudly, with confetti falling around them.

title: Making Connections
introBody: In fact books, things are often connected. A person might cause an event, or two ideas might go together. Learning to see connections helps us understand what we read!
introImage: Two colorful cartoon puzzle pieces coming together with a satisfying click, one showing a rain cloud and the other showing flowers growing.
teachBody: In a fact book, one thing can lead to another. Rain helps flowers grow. A farmer plants seeds and then food grows. When we see how things connect, we learn more!
teachImage: A chain of colorful cartoon links connecting a seed, a rain cloud, sunshine, and a blooming flower in a row, showing cause and effect.
exampleBody: A book says: Bees visit flowers. They carry pollen to other flowers. This helps new flowers grow. The connection is that bees help flowers grow by carrying pollen!
exampleImage: A friendly cartoon bee with a tiny backpack full of yellow pollen flying between two bright smiling flowers in a sunny garden.
tipBody: Look for words like "because," "so," and "then." These words often show how things are connected in a fact book!
tipImage: Three large cartoon word bubbles spelling out "because," "so," and "then" in bold colorful letters, connected by a curvy dotted line.
practiceBody: Wonderful job! Now let's practice finding connections in some fact passages. See how one thing leads to another!
practiceImage: A cartoon child drawing lines between connected pictures on a board, linking a rain cloud to a flower and a caterpillar to a butterfly, smiling proudly.

title: New Words in Facts
introBody: Fact books sometimes use words we have not heard before. These are often special words about a topic. We can use clues to figure out what they mean!
introImage: A cartoon child opening a treasure chest filled with glowing words like "habitat," "predator," and "bloom" floating out in colorful letters.
teachBody: When a fact book uses a new word, the sentences around it often explain what it means. Pictures can help too. Use these clues to figure out tricky words!
teachImage: An open fact book with a highlighted word in the center, and colorful arrows from nearby sentences and a picture all pointing toward it as clues.
exampleBody: A book says: A bear's habitat is the forest. It lives there and finds food there. What does "habitat" mean? The clues tell us a habitat is where an animal lives!
exampleImage: A cozy cartoon bear standing in front of a forest with tall trees, a river, and berries, with a sign that reads "Bear's Habitat" hanging from a branch.
tipBody: When you see a new word in a fact book, read the whole sentence again slowly. The other words around it will give you clues about what it means!
tipImage: A cartoon turtle reading a fact book very slowly and carefully, with a glowing clue trail leading from surrounding words to the tricky word in the center.
practiceBody: You are doing amazing! Now let's practice figuring out new words in fact passages. Use the clues around the word to help you!
practiceImage: A cartoon child holding a giant golden key labeled "Clues" and unlocking a padlock on a big word, with sparkles shooting out as the word's meaning is revealed.

title: Parts of a Book
introHeading: Let's Learn About Parts of a Book!
introBody: Every book has special parts. The front cover, back cover, and title page each have an important job. Let's learn what they are!
introImage: A large cartoon book standing upright and smiling, with arrows pointing to its front cover, back cover, and an open title page, each labeled in bright colors.
teachHeading: Front Cover, Back Cover, and Title Page
teachBody: The front cover shows the title and a picture. The back cover tells a little about the book. The title page is inside and shows the title, author, and illustrator!
teachImage: Three side-by-side cartoon panels showing a front cover with a title and picture, a back cover with a short paragraph, and a title page with author and illustrator names.
exampleBody: Pick up a book. The front has the title "All About Dogs" and a picture of a dog. Flip it over and the back tells you what the book is about. Open it and find the title page inside!
exampleImage: A cartoon child holding a book titled "All About Dogs" with a happy puppy on the cover, flipping it to show the back cover and then opening to the title page.
tipBody: The front cover is the first thing you see. The title page is the very first page inside. The back cover is the last thing you see when you flip the book over!
tipImage: A cartoon book with numbered arrows showing the order: 1 pointing to the front cover, 2 to the title page inside, and 3 to the back cover.
practiceBody: Nice work! Now let's practice identifying the parts of a book. You know just where to find them!
practiceImage: A cartoon child proudly holding a book and pointing to the front cover with one hand, with gold stars popping up around the book.

title: Who Made This Fact Book?
introBody: Fact books have authors and illustrators too! The author writes the facts and the illustrator draws the pictures. Let's learn about their important roles!
introImage: A cartoon author with a notebook full of facts and an illustrator with a palette of paints, standing on either side of a big fact book about space.
teachHeading: Authors and Illustrators in Fact Books
teachBody: In a fact book, the author researches and writes the information. The illustrator draws pictures or diagrams that help us understand the facts better!
teachImage: A cartoon author reading and taking notes from a big stack of research books, while an illustrator next to them paints a detailed diagram of a butterfly lifecycle.
exampleBody: A book about the ocean says "Written by Dr. Jane Wave, Illustrated by Tom Brush." Dr. Jane Wave wrote all the ocean facts. Tom Brush drew the pictures of the sea animals!
exampleImage: A cartoon book cover about the ocean with a smiling whale, showing the author name and illustrator name in clear colorful text at the bottom.
tipBody: Check the cover and the title page to find the author and illustrator. Remember: the author writes the words, the illustrator makes the pictures!
tipImage: A cartoon pencil labeled "Author" and a cartoon paintbrush labeled "Illustrator" standing on the title page of a book, each pointing to their name.
practiceBody: Super! Now let's practice finding who made different fact books. Look for the author and illustrator names!
practiceImage: A cartoon child excitedly pointing to the author and illustrator names on the cover of a colorful book about dinosaurs, with a magnifying glass in hand.

title: Pictures Help Us Learn
introBody: In fact books, pictures do more than look nice. They help explain the facts! Diagrams, photos, and drawings show us information that the words describe.
introImage: An open fact book with a labeled diagram of a plant on one page and text about how plants grow on the other page, with a cartoon child looking at both.
teachHeading: How Do Pictures Help in Fact Books?
teachBody: Pictures in fact books can show us what something looks like, how something works, or where something is. They add extra information to what the words say!
teachImage: A cartoon fact book page showing a diagram of the water cycle with arrows and labels, alongside simple text explaining rain and clouds.
exampleBody: A book says: Frogs start as tadpoles. The picture shows a tadpole, then a tadpole with legs, then a frog. The picture helps us see how a frog grows and changes!
exampleImage: A cartoon sequence showing the stages of a frog's life cycle, from a tiny egg to a tadpole to a tadpole with legs to a smiling green frog, with arrows between each stage.
tipBody: When you read a fact book, look at the pictures carefully. They might show you details that the words did not explain. Pictures and words work together!
tipImage: A cartoon pair of eyes zooming in on a detailed illustration in a fact book, with small detail callouts popping up around the picture.
practiceBody: Great job! Now let's practice using pictures to learn from fact passages. Look at both the words and the pictures!
practiceImage: A cartoon child holding an open fact book with one hand pointing to the text and the other pointing to the picture, with a lightbulb glowing above their head.

title: Reasons and Support
introBody: When an author tells us something in a fact book, they give us reasons why it is true. Finding those reasons helps us believe and understand what we read!
introImage: A cartoon author standing at a chalkboard, pointing to a big statement at the top and three reasons listed below it with colorful bullet points.
teachBody: Reasons are the facts an author uses to support a point. If the author says dogs make great pets, the reasons might be that dogs are loyal, fun, and loving!
teachImage: A cartoon dog sitting proudly next to three floating signs that read "Loyal," "Fun," and "Loving," all connected by arrows to a big banner that says "Great Pets."
exampleBody: A book says: We should drink water. It keeps us healthy. It gives us energy. It helps us think. The reasons to drink water are that it keeps us healthy, gives energy, and helps us think!
exampleImage: A cartoon glass of sparkling water with three colorful ribbons streaming out, each labeled with a reason: "Healthy," "Energy," and "Think Better."
tipBody: Look for the author's big idea first. Then find the reasons that explain why. The reasons usually come after words like "because" or are listed one by one!
tipImage: A cartoon tree with a big trunk labeled "Big Idea" and three branches, each holding a leaf labeled "Reason 1," "Reason 2," and "Reason 3."
practiceBody: Awesome learning! Now let's practice finding the reasons an author gives. Look for the facts that support the big idea!
practiceImage: A cartoon child stacking colorful blocks labeled with reasons on top of each other, building a tower under a banner that says "Author's Point."

title: Comparing Fact Books
introBody: Sometimes we read two books about the same topic. They might share some of the same facts, but they might also tell us different things. Let's learn to compare them!
introImage: Two colorful cartoon books about cats sitting side by side, one showing an orange tabby and the other showing a black kitten, with a sparkly "VS" between them.
teachBody: Two books on the same topic can tell us different facts. One book about cats might talk about what they eat. Another might talk about where they sleep. Both books teach us about cats!
teachImage: Two open cartoon books about cats, one showing a cat eating from a bowl and the other showing a cat sleeping in a bed, with a shared "Cats" label above both.
exampleBody: Book 1 says: Dogs wag their tails when happy. Book 2 says: Dogs bark to talk to people. Both are about dogs, but they tell us different things dogs do!
exampleImage: A cartoon dog wagging its tail happily on the left and the same dog barking cheerfully on the right, with two small book icons below each picture.
tipBody: After reading two books on the same topic, ask: What did both books tell me? What did only one book tell me? This helps you see what is the same and what is different!
tipImage: A cartoon child with two books open side by side on a table, drawing lines between matching facts and circling unique facts with different colored markers.
practiceBody: You are doing great! Now let's practice comparing facts from two different passages on the same topic. Let's go!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a judge's ribbon, holding two books up and comparing them with a big smile, with a scoreboard behind showing "Same" and "Different" columns.

title: Reading Left to Right
introBody: When we read, we start on the left side and move to the right. Then we go down to the next line. Let's learn the right way to follow words on a page!
introImage: A cartoon child sitting in a cozy reading nook, holding a book and tracing the words with a finger from left to right, with a dotted arrow showing the direction.
teachHeading: Left to Right, Top to Bottom
teachBody: We read words from left to right, just like how you write your name. When we finish a line, we go down to the next line. We read every page before turning to the next one!
teachImage: A big cartoon page with three lines of colorful text, and a friendly arrow character bouncing from left to right across each line, then jumping down to the next.
exampleBody: Look at this page. We start reading at the top left corner. We go across to the right. Then we go to the next line below. When we finish the page, we turn to the next one!
exampleImage: A cartoon open book with numbered arrows showing the reading path: arrow 1 goes left to right on the top line, arrow 2 drops down, arrow 3 goes left to right on the next line.
tipBody: Use your finger to follow the words as you read. Start on the left and slide your finger to the right. This helps you stay on track!
tipImage: A close-up cartoon of a child's finger pointing at and following words on a page, with a glowing trail behind the finger showing the left-to-right path.
practiceBody: Wonderful! Now let's practice reading from left to right and top to bottom. Follow the words carefully. You have got this!
practiceImage: A cartoon child confidently pointing at words on a large page, reading from left to right with a bright smile, and a row of gold stars lining the top of the page.

title: Words Are Made of Letters
introBody: Every word we say can be written down using letters. The letters go in a special order to make each word. Let's learn how spoken words become written words!
introImage: A cartoon child speaking the word "cat" in a speech bubble, and colorful letter tiles C, A, T floating down and landing in a row to spell the word.
teachBody: When we talk, we say words. When we write, we use letters to spell those words. Each word has its own special set of letters in the right order!
teachImage: A cartoon conveyor belt with individual letter blocks going in and a completed word coming out the other side, with a smiling factory character overseeing the process.
exampleBody: When you say "dog," you can write it with three letters: D-O-G. The letters D, O, and G in that order make the word "dog." Every word you say has letters that spell it!
exampleImage: Three large colorful letter blocks D, O, G sitting on a shelf next to a happy cartoon dog, with a dotted line connecting the letters to the dog.
tipBody: Point to each letter in a word as you say it. This helps you see how the letters and the sounds go together to make a word!
tipImage: A cartoon child tapping each letter of the word "sun" written on a whiteboard, with tiny sound waves coming from each letter as they point to it.
practiceBody: Great job! Now let's practice matching spoken words to their written letters. Sound out each word carefully!
practiceImage: A cartoon child at a table arranging colorful magnetic letters on a board to spell simple words, with a proud expression and sparkles around the completed words.

title: Spaces Between Words
introBody: When we write, we put spaces between words so we can read them. Without spaces, all the words would be jumbled together! Spaces help us see where one word ends and the next begins.
introImage: A cartoon sentence "I like cats" with big clear spaces between each word, and a friendly cartoon spacer character standing in each gap waving.
teachBody: Spaces in writing separate one word from the next. They make it easy to read. Without spaces, "I like cats" would look like "Ilikecats" and be very hard to read!
teachImage: A split cartoon showing "Ilikecats" all squished together on top looking confused, and "I like cats" with proper spaces on the bottom looking happy and clear.
exampleBody: Look at this: "The dog ran." There is a space between "The" and "dog" and between "dog" and "ran." Each space tells us a new word is starting!
exampleImage: The words "The dog ran" written in large colorful letters with big visible spaces between them, and tiny cartoon arrows pointing to each space.
tipBody: When you write, put a finger space between each word. Hold your finger down after one word before writing the next. This makes perfect spaces!
tipImage: A cartoon child's hand placing a finger between two words on lined paper as a spacer, with a smile drawn on the fingertip.
practiceBody: Awesome! Now let's practice finding spaces between words. Remember, each space shows where a new word starts!
practiceImage: A cartoon child happily counting the spaces between words on a big poster, with checkmarks appearing above each space they find.

title: Knowing Your Letters
introBody: The alphabet has 26 letters and each one has an uppercase and a lowercase form. Learning all your letters is the first big step to becoming a reader!
introImage: A colorful cartoon alphabet train with each car carrying a different letter, chugging through a sunny landscape with uppercase letters on top and lowercase below.
teachBody: Every letter has a big form called uppercase, like A, B, C, and a small form called lowercase, like a, b, c. They look different but they are the same letter!
teachImage: Pairs of cartoon letter characters standing side by side, a tall uppercase A next to a small lowercase a, both smiling and waving, with B/b and C/c behind them.
exampleBody: Look at the letter M. The uppercase M is tall and has two bumps. The lowercase m is small and also has two bumps. Both say the same sound!
exampleImage: A large uppercase M and a small lowercase m standing next to each other on a stage, both with smiley faces, and a speech bubble showing the "mmm" sound.
tipBody: Practice your letters every day! Sing the alphabet song and point to each letter. Try to name both the uppercase and lowercase forms!
tipImage: A cartoon child singing the alphabet song with musical notes floating around, pointing to a colorful alphabet chart on the wall.
practiceBody: You are a letter superstar! Now let's practice naming uppercase and lowercase letters. Show me what you know!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a cape with a letter A on it, flying through a sky filled with floating colorful letters, grabbing them and naming them.

title: Rhyming Words
introBody: Rhyming words are words that sound the same at the end, like "cat" and "hat." Rhyming is fun and helps us play with sounds! Let's learn more!
introImage: A cartoon cat wearing a colorful party hat, with the words "cat" and "hat" bouncing above in matching bubbly letters connected by a sparkly line.
teachBody: Words rhyme when they have the same ending sound. "Dog" and "log" rhyme. "Sun" and "fun" rhyme. Listen to the end of each word to hear if they match!
teachImage: Pairs of cartoon objects that rhyme sitting together: a dog on a log, a sun next to a child having fun, with lines connecting each rhyming pair.
exampleBody: Do "cake" and "lake" rhyme? Yes! They both end with the "ake" sound. Do "cake" and "dog" rhyme? No! They have different ending sounds.
exampleImage: A cartoon cake and a sparkling lake side by side with a big green checkmark between them, and a cake and a dog side by side with a red X between them.
tipBody: Say two words out loud and listen to the ends. If they sound the same at the end, they rhyme! Try it with your name and see what rhymes with it!
tipImage: A cartoon ear character leaning in closely to listen to two words, with colorful sound waves coming from each word and matching at the end.
practiceBody: Fun times! Now let's practice finding and making rhyming words. Listen carefully to the ending sounds!
practiceImage: A cartoon child clapping and singing, with pairs of rhyming words popping up in colorful bubbles all around them like a rhyming party.

title: Clapping Syllables
introBody: Every word is made up of parts called syllables. We can clap, stomp, or tap for each part. Counting syllables is a fun way to play with words!
introImage: A cartoon child clapping hands enthusiastically with colorful sound burst effects around the claps, and the word "butterfly" split into "but-ter-fly" above.
teachBody: A syllable is a part of a word that has one beat. "Cat" has one clap. "Tiger" has two claps: ti-ger. "Butterfly" has three claps: but-ter-fly!
teachImage: Three cartoon animals in a row: a cat with one clap symbol, a tiger with two clap symbols, and a butterfly with three clap symbols above each.
exampleBody: Let's clap the word "banana." Ba-na-na. That is three claps! Now try "dog." Just one clap! Longer words usually have more syllables than short words.
exampleImage: A cartoon banana split into three sections labeled "ba," "na," "na" with three pairs of clapping hands below, and a small cartoon dog with one clap.
tipBody: Put your hand under your chin and say a word. Count how many times your chin touches your hand. That is how many syllables the word has!
tipImage: A cartoon child with a hand under their chin saying a word, with their chin bouncing down for each syllable, and a counter showing the number going up.
practiceBody: Clap clap! Now let's practice counting syllables in different words. Clap along and count the beats!
practiceImage: A group of cartoon children standing in a line, all clapping together in rhythm, with numbers 1, 2, 3 floating above their heads for each clap.

title: Beginning and Ending Sounds
introBody: Words have a beginning sound and an ending part. We can break words apart and put them back together! This helps us read and say new words.
introImage: A cartoon word "cat" being gently pulled apart into two colorful pieces: a "c" piece and an "at" piece, with two friendly hands pulling them apart.
teachBody: The first sound in a word is called the onset. The rest of the word is called the rime. In the word "bat," the onset is "b" and the rime is "at." Together they make "bat!"
teachImage: A cartoon jigsaw puzzle with two pieces snapping together, one piece showing a large "b" and the other showing "at," forming the word "bat" with a small bat flying nearby.
exampleBody: Let's try the word "sit." The onset is "s" and the rime is "it." Now blend them together: s-it, sit! You can do this with lots of words like "hit," "bit," and "fit!"
exampleImage: Cartoon letter tiles showing "s" sliding into "it" to make "sit," with "h-it," "b-it," and "f-it" shown below as bonus examples in smaller tiles.
tipBody: Say the first sound of a word by itself, then say the rest. Now say them together fast. You just blended a word! Practice with words that end the same way.
tipImage: A cartoon rocket labeled with a beginning sound on the nose and an ending part on the body, blasting off as the two parts come together with a whoosh.
practiceBody: Great work! Now let's practice breaking words apart and putting them back together. Listen for the beginning and ending parts!
practiceImage: A cartoon child at a workbench snapping word pieces together like building blocks, with completed words stacking up on a shelf behind them.

title: Sounds in Words
introHeading: Let's Listen to Sounds in Words!
introBody: Every word is made up of individual sounds. We can hear the first sound, the middle sound, and the last sound. Listening carefully helps us read and spell!
introImage: A cartoon child cupping a hand to their ear, listening to the word "pig" with three colorful sound bubbles showing "p," "i," and "g" floating in order.
teachBody: In the word "cat," the first sound is "c," the middle sound is "a," and the last sound is "t." Every short word has these three sounds. Let's practice finding them!
teachImage: Three colorful train cars in a row labeled "First: c," "Middle: a," and "Last: t," connected together to form a word train spelling "cat."
exampleBody: What sounds are in "hop?" The first sound is "h," the middle sound is "o," and the last sound is "p." H-o-p. Now you can hear all three sounds!
exampleImage: A cartoon bunny hopping, with three stepping stones labeled "h," "o," and "p" beneath it, and the full word "hop" written in a banner above.
tipBody: Say a word slowly, stretching it out like taffy. When you say it slowly, you can hear each sound. The middle sound is the vowel, and it is usually the hardest to hear!
tipImage: A cartoon child pulling a stretchy piece of taffy with the word "map" written on it, stretching out to show "m...a...p" with each sound becoming visible.
practiceBody: You are amazing! Now let's practice hearing the first, middle, and last sounds in words. Stretch each word out slowly!
practiceImage: A cartoon child with superhero hearing powers, with big golden ear cups, listening to words and catching individual sounds in a butterfly net.

title: Changing Sounds, Changing Words
introBody: We can make new words by changing just one sound! If we change the first sound in "cat" to "b," we get "bat." Changing sounds is like word magic!
introImage: A cartoon magician waving a wand over the word "cat," and the "c" transforms into a "b" with sparkles, turning the word into "bat" with a little bat flying out.
teachHeading: Swap a Sound, Make a New Word!
teachBody: When we change one sound in a word, we make a whole new word. We can change the first sound, the middle sound, or the last sound. Each change makes something different!
teachImage: A cartoon word machine with the word "pan" going in, a dial turning the first letter, and the words "fan," "can," and "ran" coming out on colorful cards.
exampleBody: Start with the word "hat." Change the "h" to "m" and you get "mat." Change the "h" to "s" and you get "sat." One little change makes a brand new word!
exampleImage: Three cartoon hats in a row transforming: a regular hat, then an arrow to a mat on the floor, then an arrow to a child sitting down, showing hat to mat to sat.
tipBody: Think of a word and change just the first sound. How many new words can you make? Try changing "rug" to "bug," "mug," and "hug!"
tipImage: A cartoon rug with the letter "r" popping off and letters "b," "m," and "h" waiting in line to take its place, each with eager smiling faces.
practiceBody: You are a word wizard! Now let's practice making new words by changing sounds. See how many words you can create!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a wizard hat, tapping words with a star wand and watching them transform into new words in a shower of colorful sparkles.

title: Letters and Their Sounds
introBody: Every letter of the alphabet makes a sound. When we know what sound each letter makes, we can read words! Let's learn about letter sounds together.
introImage: A colorful cartoon alphabet poster on a wall with each letter paired with a picture: A with an apple, B with a ball, C with a cat, and so on.
teachBody: Each letter makes its own special sound. The letter S says "sss" like a snake. The letter M says "mmm" like when food is yummy. Knowing these sounds helps us read!
teachImage: A cartoon letter S shaped like a snake saying "sss" and a cartoon letter M shaped like mountains with a child saying "mmm" while eating a cookie.
exampleBody: The letter B makes the "b" sound, like in "ball" and "bear." The letter T makes the "t" sound, like in "top" and "turtle." Every letter has a sound you can hear in words!
exampleImage: A large cartoon letter B bouncing a ball and hugging a bear, and a large cartoon letter T spinning a top and riding a turtle, all in a colorful playground.
tipBody: Think of a word that starts with each letter to help you remember its sound. B is for ball, C is for cat, D is for dog. Make your own letter-sound list!
tipImage: A cartoon child making a colorful flip book with each page showing a letter and a picture of a word that starts with that letter's sound.
practiceBody: Terrific! Now let's practice matching letters to their sounds. Listen to the sound and find the right letter!
practiceImage: A cartoon child at a matching game board, drawing lines between letters on one side and pictures of objects on the other side, with happy matches lighting up.

title: Vowel Sounds
introBody: The letters A, E, I, O, and U are special letters called vowels. Each vowel can make a short sound and a long sound. Let's learn both!
introImage: Five large colorful cartoon letters A, E, I, O, U standing on a stage like rock stars, each wearing a crown and glowing with two different colored auras.
teachBody: Vowels have two sounds. Short A says "a" like in "cat." Long A says its name, like in "cake." Short O says "o" like in "hot." Long O says its name, like in "home!"
teachImage: A cartoon toggle switch with two settings for each vowel: a short sound side with example words and a long sound side with example words, brightly colored.
exampleBody: The letter I makes two sounds. Short I sounds like "i" in "pig." Long I says its name like "i" in "bike." Listen for the difference: pig and bike!
exampleImage: A cartoon pig riding a bicycle, with the short "i" sound labeled near the pig and the long "i" sound labeled near the bike, connected by a curvy road.
tipBody: When a vowel says its name, it is making the long sound. When it makes a shorter, different sound, it is the short sound. Try saying both and hear the difference!
tipImage: A cartoon vowel character holding two masks, one labeled "Short" making a quick sound and one labeled "Long" saying its own name, like a theater performer.
practiceBody: Excellent! Now let's practice hearing short and long vowel sounds. Listen carefully and pick the right sound!
practiceImage: A cartoon child with headphones on, listening to words and sorting them into two buckets labeled "Short Vowel" and "Long Vowel" with a focused smile.

title: Sight Words
introBody: Sight words are words we see a lot when we read. Words like "the," "is," "and," and "you" are everywhere! Learning to read them quickly makes reading much easier.
introImage: A cartoon road with sight words written on signs along the way: "the," "is," "you," "my," "she," and a happy cartoon child walking along the path reading each sign.
teachBody: Sight words appear in almost every book. When you can read them fast without sounding them out, reading becomes smoother and more fun. Practice makes them easy!
teachImage: A cartoon speed reader character zooming past words like "the," "of," "to," "and," recognizing each one instantly with little lightning bolt effects.
exampleBody: Look at this sentence: "She is my friend." The words "she," "is," and "my" are all sight words. You will see them in lots and lots of books!
exampleImage: A cartoon sentence "She is my friend" floating in the air with the sight words "she," "is," and "my" glowing in bright gold while the other words are in blue.
tipBody: Make flashcards for sight words and practice reading them fast. The more you see them, the faster you will recognize them. Try to read them in one second each!
tipImage: A cartoon child flipping through colorful flashcards at a desk, with sight words zooming by and a small timer showing one second per card.
practiceBody: You are a sight word champion! Now let's practice reading sight words quickly. See how many you know!
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing on a winner's podium holding a trophy labeled "Sight Word Star," surrounded by floating sight words and cheering letter characters.

title: Look-Alike Words
introBody: Some words look almost the same, like "cat" and "cap." They have the same letters except for one! Learning to tell them apart helps us read the right word.
introImage: Two cartoon words "cat" and "cap" standing next to each other like twins, with the differing letters "t" and "p" highlighted in different bright colors.
teachBody: Words like "bat" and "bag" look similar because they start with the same letters. But the last letter is different, and that changes the sound and the meaning of the word!
teachImage: A cartoon bat and a cartoon bag side by side, with their spellings shown below. The matching letters "b" and "a" are in green, and the different letters "t" and "g" are in red.
exampleBody: Look at "pin" and "pan." They both start with "p" and end with "n." But the middle letter is different: "i" in pin and "a" in pan. That changes the word!
exampleImage: A cartoon sewing pin next to a cartoon cooking pan, with their spellings shown and the middle letter of each word circled in a bright contrasting color.
tipBody: When two words look alike, point to each letter one by one. Find the letter that is different. Say both words and listen for how the sounds change!
tipImage: A cartoon detective child with a magnifying glass comparing two words letter by letter on a chalkboard, with the different letter circled and glowing.
practiceBody: Sharp eyes! Now let's practice telling look-alike words apart. Find the letter that is different and read each word!
practiceImage: A cartoon child at a game show buzzer, confidently pressing the button after spotting the difference between two similar words on a big screen.

title: Reading with Purpose
introBody: Reading is not just saying words. Real reading means understanding what the words are telling you! When we read with purpose, we think about what the words mean.
introImage: A cartoon child sitting in a cozy armchair reading a small book, with a glowing thought bubble above showing the story scene they are imagining.
teachBody: When you read, think about what is happening. Ask yourself: What is this about? Does it make sense? If something does not make sense, go back and read it again!
teachImage: A cartoon child reading a sentence on a page, with a thought bubble showing a lightbulb turning on as they understand the meaning of the words.
exampleBody: You read: "The cat sat on the mat." You understand that a cat is sitting on a mat. You can picture it in your mind! That means you are reading with purpose.
exampleImage: A cartoon child reading the words "The cat sat on the mat" and above their head a vivid thought bubble shows a fluffy cartoon cat sitting on a colorful mat.
tipBody: After you read a sentence, close your eyes and try to picture what it says. If you can see it in your mind, you understood it! If not, read it one more time.
tipImage: A cartoon child with closed eyes and a peaceful smile, with a colorful imagination cloud above showing a scene from their book coming to life.
practiceBody: Wonderful! Now let's practice reading and understanding short passages. Think about what the words mean as you read!
practiceImage: A cartoon child reading a small book with understanding, surrounded by floating scenes from the story materializing in colorful bubbles around them.

title: Grammar Basics
introBody: Grammar is the set of rules that help us speak and write so others can understand us. Using good grammar means putting words together the right way!
introImage: A cartoon classroom with a friendly teacher character pointing to a sentence on the board, with smiling word characters standing in the correct order.
teachBody: When we speak or write, we use nouns, which are naming words like "dog" or "Mom." We also use verbs, which are action words like "run" or "jump." Together they make sentences!
teachImage: A cartoon dog labeled "Noun" jumping over a fence labeled "Verb," with the complete sentence "The dog jumps" written in a banner above them.
exampleBody: "The girl runs fast." "Girl" is a noun because it names a person. "Runs" is a verb because it tells what the girl does. Together they make a clear sentence!
exampleImage: A cartoon girl running fast on a track, with the word "girl" labeled in blue under her and the word "runs" labeled in red under her moving feet.
tipBody: Nouns are the who or what. Verbs are what they do. Find the noun and verb in a sentence and you will understand it! Ask: Who or what? and What did they do?
tipImage: Two cartoon magnifying glasses, a blue one labeled "Who?" focusing on a noun and a red one labeled "What did they do?" focusing on a verb in a sentence.
practiceBody: Great work! Now let's practice using nouns and verbs in sentences. Put your words together the right way!
practiceImage: A cartoon child arranging word tiles on a table to build sentences, with completed sentences lighting up in green and a satisfied smile on their face.

title: Capitals and Punctuation
introHeading: Let's Learn About Capitals and Punctuation!
introBody: When we write, we use capital letters and punctuation marks to make our writing clear. Capital letters start sentences and punctuation marks end them!
introImage: A cartoon sentence with a big bold capital letter at the start and a smiling period character at the end, both wearing top hats and looking proud.
teachBody: Every sentence starts with a capital letter. Names start with capital letters too! At the end of a sentence, we use a period, a question mark, or an exclamation point.
teachImage: A cartoon uppercase A standing tall at the beginning of a sentence, and three punctuation characters at the end: a period, a question mark, and an exclamation point waving.
exampleBody: "My name is Sam." The M in My is a capital because it starts the sentence. The S in Sam is a capital because it is a name. The period shows the sentence is done!
exampleImage: The sentence "My name is Sam." written in large letters, with the capital M and capital S glowing in gold, and the period glowing in red at the end.
tipBody: Remember: Big letter at the beginning, little dot at the end! Every sentence needs a capital letter to start and a punctuation mark to finish.
tipImage: A cartoon gate with a capital letter as the entrance on the left and a period as the exit on the right, with word characters walking through in a sentence.
practiceBody: Nice! Now let's practice using capital letters and punctuation. Give every sentence a capital start and a proper ending!
practiceImage: A cartoon child at a desk writing a sentence with a big smile, carefully making the first letter a capital and putting a period at the end with a sparkle.

title: Figuring Out Word Meanings
introBody: When you come across a word you do not know, there are smart ways to figure out what it means. You can use clues from the sentence, the pictures, or even parts of the word!
introImage: A cartoon child standing in front of a giant word with a question mark above their head, surrounded by floating clue bubbles from sentences, pictures, and word parts.
teachHeading: Clues Help Us Learn New Words
teachBody: You can figure out a new word by looking at the words around it, looking at pictures, or asking what makes sense in the sentence. These are all great clues!
teachImage: Three cartoon toolbox items: a magnifying glass for context clues, a picture frame for picture clues, and a puzzle piece for word parts, all glowing and helpful.
exampleBody: A sentence says: "The tiny kitten fit in my hand." What does "tiny" mean? If the kitten fits in a hand, it must be very small. "Tiny" means very small!
exampleImage: A cartoon hand gently holding an adorable very small kitten, with the word "tiny" in sparkly letters floating above, and an arrow pointing to the small size.
tipBody: Try replacing the new word with a word you know and see if the sentence still makes sense. If it does, you probably figured out the meaning!
tipImage: A cartoon word bubble with a mystery word being swapped out for a known word, and a sentence below showing both versions with a checkmark next to the one that works.
practiceBody: Smart thinking! Now let's practice figuring out what new words mean. Use all your clues to solve each one!
practiceImage: A cartoon child dressed as a detective with a notepad, cracking the code of new vocabulary words, with solved words stacking up like trophies behind them.

title: Word Relationships
introBody: Words are connected in fun ways! Some words mean the same thing, some mean the opposite, and some words go together. Learning how words relate helps us use them better!
introImage: A cartoon web of colorful words connected by sparkling lines, with pairs like "hot-cold," "big-large," and groups like "apple, banana, orange" clustered together.
teachBody: Some words are opposites, like "big" and "small." Some words mean almost the same thing, like "happy" and "glad." Some words belong in the same group, like "red," "blue," and "green!"
teachImage: Three cartoon sections: a seesaw with "big" on one end and "small" on the other, two smiling faces labeled "happy" and "glad," and a paint palette with color names.
exampleBody: "Hot" and "cold" are opposites. "Happy" and "glad" mean almost the same thing. "Dog," "cat," and "fish" all belong in the group called "animals."
exampleImage: A cartoon thermometer showing hot on top and cold on bottom, two happy faces high-fiving labeled "happy" and "glad," and three animals grouped in a circle labeled "Animals."
tipBody: Think about how words are connected. Are they opposites? Do they mean the same thing? Do they belong in the same group? Sorting words this way helps you learn more!
tipImage: A cartoon child sorting word cards into three colorful bins labeled "Opposites," "Same Meaning," and "Same Group," with cards flying into the right bins.
practiceBody: Wonderful! Now let's practice exploring how words are related. Sort, match, and group the words!
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing in a room full of floating word bubbles, happily connecting related words with colorful ribbons and watching them snap together.

title: Using New Words
introBody: Every day we learn new words from talking, reading, and listening. The more words we know, the better we can share our ideas! Let's practice using our new words.
introImage: A cartoon child surrounded by floating colorful words like "enormous," "delicious," "sparkling," and "cozy" that they have collected in a basket like butterflies.
teachBody: When you hear a new word in a story or a conversation, try using it yourself! The more you practice new words, the easier they become. Every new word makes you a stronger reader!
teachImage: A cartoon garden where words are growing like flowers, with a child watering them. Small seedling words are growing into big blooming word flowers.
exampleBody: You learned the word "enormous" in a story. Now try using it! Instead of saying "The elephant is big," you can say "The elephant is enormous!" Great job using a new word!
exampleImage: A cartoon child pointing excitedly at an enormous cartoon elephant, with a speech bubble saying "The elephant is enormous!" and the word "enormous" sparkling.
tipBody: Keep a word wall or word jar at home. Every time you learn a new word, write it down. Try to use one new word every day when you talk or write!
tipImage: A cartoon glass jar filled with colorful word strips, with a child pulling one out that says "wonderful" and using it in a speech bubble.
practiceBody: Amazing! Now let's practice choosing and using the best words. Show off all the great words you have learned!
practiceImage: A cartoon child on a stage with a microphone, confidently using big colorful words that float up like fireworks, with an audience of stuffed animals cheering.
